chunk_summary: Guidelines for empiric antibiotic regimens in severe acute malnutrition (SAM) focus on infection management, renal safety, and step-down criteria for transitioning from IV/IM to oral therapy based on clinical improvement and nutritional recovery.
learning_objectives: Understand empiric antibiotic regimens for SAM, including dosages and administration routes.
Learn criteria for transitioning from inpatient to outpatient care in SAM management.
Recognize renal safety protocols and step-down rules for antibiotics like gentamicin.
Identify escalation strategies for worsening clinical status or septic shock in SAM.
key_facts: Oral amoxicillin 15 mg/kg every 8 hours for 5 days is recommended for uncomplicated outpatient SAM.
First-line inpatient SAM regimen: Ampicillin 50 mg/kg every 6 hours plus gentamicin 7.5 mg/kg once daily for 7 days.
Add cloxacillin 100 mg/kg/day in 4 doses for staphylococcal infections with skin lesions or abscesses.
Escalation options for septic shock: Cefotaxime 150 mg/kg/day in 3 doses or ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg/day in 2 doses.
Withhold gentamicin if oliguria/anuria occurs; reassess hydration, perfusion, and renal function before restarting.
Step down to oral therapy when the child is clinically improving, tolerating feeds, and can complete the course reliably.
reference_quote: Empiric antibiotics in SAM must be 'ready to prescribe,' because invasive infection can present without fever and delays increase mortality. Align doses to one local standard (e.g., MoHFW/NHM facility-based guidance) and document renal-safety stop rules, especially for gentamicin.
document_key_topics: Renal safety protocols in SAM management
Escalation strategies for septic shock in SAM
